claim: Maquis (upland Barrocal) is virtually impenetrable to a human or dog — densely packed shrubs and trees with many spiny species — making it a high-containment zone where lost dogs are extremely difficult to find or self-extricate.
stance_target: maquis as barrier/containment zone in territorial model
scope_conditions: upland Barrocal, higher elevations toward Monchique
quoted_support: not only are the plants so densely packed together as to make the habitat virtually impenetrable, but many of the trees and shrubs are spiny, and others, such as Gum Cistus (Cistus ladanifer), are extremely sticky.

claim: Garrigue (lowland Barrocal near coast) is open and walkable with bare stony ground between plant cushions — the most permeable vegetation zone for a searching human or traveling dog.
stance_target: garrigue as high-permeability zone in terrain model
scope_conditions: near-coastal barrocal, between beaches and inland fertile ground
quoted_support: This strange habitat is best recognised as an area of stony, inhospitable-looking ground but which nevertheless in near-coastal parts supports numerous low-growing pincushions of vividly flowering plants with convenient bare patches for walking in between.

claim: The Barrocal landscape undergoes extreme seasonal appearance change — green and flowering in spring/early summer, completely brown and barren by July-August — with implications for dog visibility and scent detection across seasons.
stance_target: seasonal encoding requirement in territorial model
quoted_support: Once the flowering time is over and the real summer heat arrives, the plants survive by dying back completely from the surface of the ground... The Algarve landscape in July and August is so barren and brown that it is hard to believe the riot of colour that has existed during spring and early summer.

claim: Garrigue habitat lies 'just behind the coast in between the beaches and the more fertile ground further inland' — confirming the precise geographic band where open, traversable terrain transitions to the agricultural/fertile interior.
stance_target: coastal garrigue band as geographic transition marker for dog-movement modeling
quoted_support: Garrigue habitats are easy to find in the Algarve and most often lie just behind the coast in between the beaches and the more fertile ground further inland.

claim: Maquis is more common in western Algarve than further east or in Iberian Peninsula due to higher rainfall, making the western Serra and upper Barrocal particularly impenetrable terrain for lost dog searches.
stance_target: west Algarve maquis as higher-impenetrability zone than east
scope_conditions: Algarve maquis vs broader Iberian Peninsula
quoted_support: It is much more common in the Algarve than further east in the Iberian Peninsula and beyond to the islands of Greece because the Algarve benefits from a higher level of rainfall.

claim: Snakes and lizards are found in both garrigue and maquis, but much harder to detect in maquis — paralleling the difficulty a lost dog would have navigating or being tracked through dense maquis vs open garrigue.
stance_target: habitat permeability as proxy for dog detectability
quoted_support: The shelter provided by the dense thicket of shrubs and trees also makes it an ideal place for snakes and lizards although they are much harder to see there than they are in the more open ground of the Garrigue.
